one_line_takeaway: XekRung-8B, an Alibaba-built Qwen3-8B model specialized via CPT+SFT+multi-task-RL (including RLVR with explicit CWE/CVSS/ATT&CK reward functions and adversarial self-evolution), reports 81.04% average across 15 cybersecurity knowledge/prediction benchmarks (+9.03pp over its Qwen3-8B base, beating the larger SecGPT-14B by >12pp) while losing only ~0.8pp on general-purpose benchmarks, but the report omits results for its own headline agentic/CTF/exploit capabilities and any ablation or safety-evaluation data.
key_finding: XekRung-8B achieves an overall average of 81.04% across the 15 cybersecurity benchmarks, a +9.03 percentage-point improvement over its own base model Qwen3-8B (72.01%), and outperforms all evaluated same-scale and larger baselines on this composite score: Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning 68.54%, Llama-Primus-Reasoning-8B 62.88%, SecGPT-14B 68.21% (XekRung-8B exceeds it by >12.8 points despite SecGPT-14B having ~1.75x the parameters), Qwen3-14B 77.32%, Qwen3.5-9B 77.29%, and even Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct 77.61% (8.75x the parameters). Per-benchmark, the largest domain gains over the Qwen3-8B base are on CTIBench-ATE (73.39 vs. 25.41) and CTIBench-MCQ (70.52 vs. 37.64); on CTIBench-VSP, XekRung-8B scores 92.12 versus SecGPT-14B's 3.71 (the paper attributes such near-zero baseline scores to 'output-format incompatibility' for some models/benchmarks, flagged with a dash convention elsewhere in the general-benchmark table, though this specific VSP entry for SecGPT-14B is reported as a numeric 3.71 rather than a dash). On the 35-benchmark general-purpose suite, XekRung-8B retains 72.54% overall versus the Qwen3-8B base's 73.31%, i.e., a roughly 0.8-point degradation from the full CPT->SFT->RL security specialization.
core_contribution: XekRung Technical Report (Alibaba Security AGI Lab, arXiv:2605.00072, 4 May 2026) presents XekRung, a cybersecurity-specialized LLM built on the Qwen3-8B backbone via a complete continued-pretraining (CPT) -> supervised fine-tuning (SFT) -> multi-task reinforcement-learning (RL) pipeline. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a domain-specific data-engineering system for pretraining (explanatory annotation of low-level security artifacts such as disassembly/PCAP/YARA/eBPF traces, CVE-anchored correlated-information aggregation with primacy-bias-aware reordering, entropy-guided long-context synthesis built on EntropyLong, and code-evolution-based training data covering diffs, commit trajectories, and self-correction traces); (2) a mid-training stage injecting long-horizon agentic trajectories (via a sigmoid-scheduled mixing curriculum) and repository-level code context before SFT; (3) a post-training pipeline unifying GRPO-based multi-task RL (RLVR with three explicit cybersecurity reward functions -- CWE root-cause mapping, CVSS severity prediction, and MITRE ATT&CK technique-extraction F1 -- plus Agentic RL and the token-localized preference algorithm ActionRL) with two self-evolution loops (an adversarial red-team/target-model exploit-vs-patch loop adapted from SEAS/Code-A1, and a vulnerability writer-vs-detector self-play loop); and (4) a two-stage knowledge-distillation pipeline (black-box cold start + on-policy white-box distillation using the ABKD alpha-beta-divergence loss) producing compact (<=4B) variants. Several named algorithmic components (GRPO/Shao et al. 2024, EntropyLong/Jia et al. 2025, NExtLong/Gao et al. 2025, SEAS/Diao et al. 2025, ABKD/Wang et al. 2025a, ActionRL/Qoder Team 2026, PCGrad/Yu et al. 2020) are adopted from prior work, not introduced by this paper; the paper's contribution is their integration and adaptation into a single cybersecurity training pipeline plus the associated data-construction strategies and evaluation protocol.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat-model paper in the classical AI-security-KG sense; it is a capability/training report for a dual-use cybersecurity-domain LLM. The closest analogue to a threat model appears in the self-evolution mechanisms: an internal Red-Team model attempts to execute sandboxed vulnerability exploits against a Target model that must produce functional patches to block them (reward given to the Red Team only on successful exploit execution, to the Target only on successful blocking patch), and a parallel 'vulnerability writer vs. detector' self-play loop where the writer crafts exploits designed to evade the detector's heuristics. Both are internal training-time adversarial games used to generate supervision, not a description of external attackers or deployment-time threats to the model itself. The paper explicitly contrasts XekRung's stated design goals with 'Claude Mythos Preview,' described in the paper as exhibiting 'emergent offensive capabilities such as zero-day discovery and autonomous exploit chain construction,' and 'GPT-5.4-Cyber,' described as fine-tuned for defensive analysis with relaxed refusal boundaries -- these are the paper's characterizations of cited proprietary systems, not independently verified by this reader. The report does not include a dedicated safety/misuse-mitigation section, red-teaming-for-harm results, or a statement on model release/weight availability; a 'Safety Compliance' score is listed only as one of four dimensions scored by an internal LLM-as-judge Data Quality Scoring Model used to filter training data, not as a deployment safeguard.
limitations: (1) Self-published technical report from an industry lab (Alibaba Security AGI Lab), not peer-reviewed; all benchmark numbers are self-reported and evaluated under the authors' 'unified evaluation framework' with no third-party reproduction. (2) The paper explicitly states that agentic and code-level cybersecurity benchmarks are 'under active integration' and were NOT included in the reported 15-benchmark suite -- the reported results are multiple-choice/knowledge/prediction tasks, not evaluations of the paper's own headline agentic capabilities (automated penetration testing, CTF solving, vulnerability remediation) which are described only qualitatively. (3) The paper states it 'conduct[s] systematic ablations' over data type and data provenance mixture ratios (Section 3.1.4) but presents no ablation tables, numbers, or figures for these claims in the report -- the mixture-selection methodology is asserted, not evidenced. (4) No information on model/weight release, code availability, or reproducibility artifacts is given anywhere in the report. (5) No dedicated red-teaming-for-harm, misuse-potential, or safety-evaluation section despite the model's stated dual-use capabilities (exploit construction, self-play vulnerability writing); the only safety-related mechanism described is an internal training-data quality filter dimension, not a deployment or release safeguard. (6) Several core algorithmic components (GRPO, EntropyLong, NExtLong, SEAS, ABKD, ActionRL, PCGrad) are adopted wholesale from cited external work; the report's novelty lies in their combination and in the domain-specific data pipeline, not in new algorithms. (7) Comparison baselines were evaluated by the authors themselves rather than pulled from each baseline's own published numbers, raising the possibility of evaluation-protocol mismatches favoring XekRung (e.g., prompt format, few-shot count) -- the paper does not address this risk.

datasets_used: CPT corpus: multi-source cybersecurity data (open-source/web content, internal product data, internal knowledge documents, security telemetry logs, CVE-annotated code) -- reported in the paper's Table 1 comparison row as 5 billion tokens
SFT corpus: ~300k curated instruction instances (cybersecurity + general)
RL corpus: ~120k high-complexity prompts, 70% security-specific / 30% general reasoning

35 general-purpose benchmarks across 9 categories (e.g., MMLU, MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, ARC-Challenge, GPQA, Xiezhi-EN/CN, TriviaQA, NarrativeQA, OpenBookQA, SQuAD 2.0, LAMBADA, StoryCloze, BoolQ, BBH, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, PIQA, SocialIQA, CommonsenseQA, CSEM, CHID, WPLC, CMMLU, C-Eval, C3, CLUEWSC, OCNLI, MATH, TAL-SCQ5K-EN/CN, DROP, TheoremQA, GSM8K-ZH, Ape210K)

15 cybersecurity benchmarks: CSE-Benchmark, CTIBench-MCQ, CTIBench-RCM, CTIBench-VSP, CTIBench-ATE, MMLU-Computer-Security, SecEval, SecBench-MCQ, CyberMetric-10kQ, CyberMetric-2kQ, CyberMetric-500Q, CyberMetric-80Q, CISSP-MC-ZH, CISSP-SC-EN, CISSP-SC-ZH
quantitative_results: Cybersecurity overall average: XekRung-8B 81.04% vs. Qwen3-8B base 72.01% (+9.03pp)

Cybersecurity overall average: XekRung-8B 81.04% vs. Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning 68.54%, Llama-Primus-Reasoning-8B 62.88%, SecGPT-14B 68.21%, Qwen3-14B 77.32%, Qwen3.5-9B 77.29%, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct 77.61%, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 61.93%
General-purpose overall average: XekRung-8B 72.54% vs. Qwen3-8B base 73.31%
CTIBench-ATE: XekRung-8B 73.39 vs. Qwen3-8B 25.41, Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning 54.45, SecGPT-14B 14.80
CTIBench-RCM: XekRung-8B 77.30 vs. Qwen3-8B 54.50, SecGPT-14B 77.10 (closely matched)
CISSP-MC-ZH: XekRung-8B 57.71 vs. Qwen3-8B 55.81, Qwen3.5-9B 60.99 (highest among ≤14B)
